content.problemStatement:
The Mean Absolute Difference is a measure of statistical dispersion equal to the average absolute difference of two independent values drawn from a probability distribution.

In this problem, we are concerned not with Mean Absolute Difference (MAD) of two distributions but rather with the Summed Absolute Difference (SAD) of two arrays. Given arrays (1-indexed) **P** and **Q** of lengths **p** and **q**, define

SAD(P,Q)=∑i=1p∑j=1q∣Pi−Qj∣\text{SAD}(P, Q) = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \sum_{j=1}^{q} |P_i - Q_j|SAD(P,Q)=i=1∑p​j=1∑q​∣Pi​−Qj​∣Given a collection of **K** arrays **A1**, ..., **AK**, report SAD(**Ai**, **Aj**) for several queries (**i**, **j**).


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single positive integer **K**.
Each of the next **K** lines starts with a positive integer **si**, the size of **Ai**, followed by **si** space-separated integers **Ai,1**, ..., **Ai,si** which denotes the array **Ai**.
The next line of input consists of a single positive integer **M**.
Each of the next **M** lines consists of 2 positive integers **i** and **j** which lie between 1 and **K** (inclusive), specifying the indices of the arrays for which the SAD value must be reported.


Output Format
Output **M** lines. Line **i** should contain the answer to query **i**.
constraints: 1 ≤ i, j ≤ K in each of the queries
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single positive integer K, the number of arrays. Each of the next K lines starts with a positive integer si, the size of Ai, followed by si space-separated integers Ai,1, ..., Ai,si which denotes the array Ai. The next line of input consists of a single positive integer M, the number of queries. Each of the next M lines consists of 2 positive integers i and j which lie between 1 and K (inclusive), specifying the indices of the arrays for which the SAD value must be reported.
content.outputFormat: Output M lines. Line i should contain the answer to query i.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we first read in all the arrays and store them. Then, for each query, we calculate the Summed Absolute Difference (SAD) between the two specified arrays. This is done by iterating over each element in the first array and each element in the second array, calculating the absolute difference between the two elements, and summing up these differences.
solution.timeComplexity: O(K * max(si)^2 + M * max(si) * max(sj)) — the first term accounts for reading in the arrays and the second term accounts for calculating the SAD for each query
solution.spaceComplexity: O(K * max(si)) — this is the space needed to store all the arrays

code.Python3:
import sys
K = int(sys.stdin.readline())
arrays = []
for _ in range(K):
    si, *ai = map(int, sys.stdin.readline().split())
    arrays.append(ai)
M = int(sys.stdin.readline())
for _ in range(M):
    a, b = map(int, sys.stdin.readline().split())
    a -= 1; b -= 1
    sad = sum(abs(x - y) for x in arrays[a] for y in arrays[b])
    print(sad)
